request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"vine::social_communication","name":"vine","one_liner":"Short-form looping video platform acquired by Twitter that failed when creator monetization was neglected and competitors replicated the format at scale.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":7,"rationale":"Both are free, ad-supported B2C social apps targeting consumers with a free product. Vine never successfully monetized ads at scale, and the new pitch also relies on ad_revenue from a free product. The core monetization logic is identical; the difference is medium (video vs. voice) and the new pitch's tighter geographic scope (college towers vs. global)."},"market":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both target the consumer social/entertainment market and rely on viral, short-form user-generated content loops. Vine skewed young (Gen Z/millennial), which overlaps with college audiences. However, Vine was a global platform while the pitch targets a hyper-local college segment, making the addressable market much smaller."},"gtm":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both depend on organic virality and UGC flywheel dynamics — Vine went viral in App Store charts, the new pitch is viral in college towers. Both are iOS/Android apps distributed freely. The new pitch's explicitly location-anchored virality (dorm towers) is more constrained and campus-by-campus, unlike Vine's broad social graph distribution via Twitter embeds."},"stage_scale":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both are early-stage consumer social apps with free products, zero revenue at launch, and reliance on network effects to grow before monetizing. Vine had the backing of Twitter after four months; the new pitch appears to be at an independent seed stage with a narrower initial footprint."}},"why_similar":"Both are free, ad-supported consumer social apps built around bite-sized user-generated content clips, relying on network effects and virality among young audiences for growth. The core business model — free product, ad revenue, consumer social — is identical. Both face the same cold-start problem and the same dependency on retaining top creators to keep the feed valuable.","where_diverged":"Three concrete dimensions of divergence: (1) Medium — the new pitch uses 30-second voice notes, not 6-second looping video, which is a fundamentally different content format and engagement mechanic. (2) Geography/scope — the new pitch is deliberately hyper-local, anchored to a single recurring physical location (college towers), whereas Vine pursued global scale from day one. (3) Independence — the new pitch is apparently founder-owned at seed stage, while Vine was acquired by Twitter at pre-launch and its fate was therefore entirely governed by Twitter's strategic priorities and cost-cutting decisions.","failure_causes":["acquired by Twitter, losing strategic autonomy","no creator monetization program, driving top creators to YouTube and Instagram","competitor feature replication by Instagram and Snapchat eroded differentiation","Twitter's broader cost-cutting and strategic misalignment","over-reliance on Twitter distribution rather than independent growth channels","6-second format too restrictive for evolving creator needs","failure to retain power users who drove disproportionate content volume"],"lessons_for_input":["Build a robust creator monetization layer early — do not assume ad revenue alone will retain top content producers.","Maintain platform independence; if you take acquisition offers, negotiate contractual protections for creator tools and platform continuity.","Differentiate your content format aggressively from incumbent short-form platforms so competitors cannot replicate your core feature trivially.","Validate that your hyper-local virality (college towers) can translate to broader networks before betting on ad revenue at scale — local density does not automatically become national reach.","Establish direct relationships with your top creators before they accumulate enough following to self-distribute on larger platforms."]}
